sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Acute Decompensated Heart Failure (ADHF) is the sudden or gradual worsening of signs and symptoms of heart failure, resulting in acute respiratory distress and/or systemic congestion. It often occurs in patients with pre-existing chronic heart failure but can also be the initial presentation of cardiac dysfunction from a myocardial infarction (MI) for example. ADHF can be a result of right (peripheral), left (pulmonary) or biventricular failure.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
Right Ventricular Failure Left Ventricular Failure Additional Signs & Symptoms
- Peripheral Oedema
- Hypotension
- Jugular vein distention
- Hepatomegaly (enlarged liver)
- Ascites (in advanced cases)
- Acute Dyspnoea (SOB)
- Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea
- Orthopnoea (worse lying flat)
- Tachypnoea
- Crepes on auscultation
- Hypoxia or low o2 saturation
- Pink frothy sputum
- Chest discomfort (MI, ischemia possible)
- Tachycardia
- Cool, clammy skin
- Cyanosis
- Altered mental status
- Cardiac Arrhythmia
- Lethargy

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Right-sided failure or preload-dependent states may deteriorate with nitrates, so avoid them unless the diagnosis and haemodynamics clearly support their use
- Oxygen should be given only if indicated by hypoxaemia or significant respiratory distress
- Confusion or reduced conscious state may reflect hypoxia, fatigue, or poor perfusion
- Use clinical judgment to differentiate ADHF from sepsis, pneumonia, asthma or COPD, pulmonary embolus, ACS, or other causes of dyspnoea
- Do not allow the patient to walk if acutely breathless or unstable
- Important precipitants include ACS, dysrhythmia, uncontrolled hypertension, medication non-adherence, renal impairment, infection, valvular disease, and fluid overload

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Open, clear, and maintain the airway
- Administer oxygen and ventilatory support if required, titrating oxygen to clinical need rather than using it routinely in non-hypoxaemic patients
- If blood pressure permits, position sitting or semi-recumbent in a position of comfort
- Perform repeated vital signs and ECG monitoring
- Escalate early if the patient is tiring, hypotensive, or has markedly increased work of breathing

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Obtain a 12 lead ECG
- Establish vascular access
- Consider fluid therapy only if hypovolaemia is clearly indicated, using cautious aliquots and frequent reassessment
- Consider aspirin if ACS is suspected and there is no clear contraindication
- Consider GTN in hypertensive acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema if blood pressure is adequate and there is no suspected preload-dependent state, in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Consider loop diuretic therapy in consultation with the on-call doctor when fluid overload is evident and the clinical picture supports it
- Consider non-invasive ventilatory support where available, appropriate, and within local protocol
- Manage the likely precipitant in line with the relevant guideline
